narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—specifically, a 53% chance of not passing. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: the strongest signal was your Year 3 decile of 2.0, which stood out as the biggest pull toward potential struggles, suggesting those later law school grades didn't climb as the model expects for bar success. Layered on that, your LSAT score of 25.5 echoed a similar cautionary note, as it fell short of the benchmarks that typically light the way to passing. Your Year 1 decile of 3.0 added to this foundation, painting an early picture of academic hurdles that carried forward. Even with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.1 and middle family income, those factors nudged the outlook downward rather than lifting it, especially since the model sees middle-income backgrounds sometimes facing extra barriers without the cushions higher brackets provide. Your background as a white female in a full-time program didn't shift the tide strongly either, as these elements aligned more with patterns of challenge in the data we've seen. It wasn't any one thing, but this combination—like a steady current—told the model your story might veer toward that 53% failure risk, leading to the tough call on your application. The good news? You're full of potential, and focusing on boosting those deciles or LSAT-like metrics could rewrite this narrative for the next opportunity. You've got this—keep pushing.
